C# Migration at Scale

Moderne + Billtrust

PROOF OF CONCEPT
March 2026
The Timeline

.NET 8 and .NET 9 both reach EOL November 10, 2026.

Two supported .NET versions expire on the same date — a convergence event that affects every .NET shop. Every codebase on .NET 8 or .NET 9 must migrate to .NET 10.

.NET 6 (LTS)
EOL Nov 12, 2024
.NET 7 (STS)
EOL May 14, 2024
.NET 8 (LTS) ← EOL
EOL Nov 10, 2026 — 8 months away
.NET 9 (STS) ← EOL
EOL Nov 10, 2026 — 8 months away
.NET 10 (LTS) ✓
Released Nov 2025 — target version
What Manual Migration Costs
  • Breaking changes compound. A .NET 6 → 10 migration traverses 4 major versions, each with its own set of API removals, renames, and behavioral changes.
  • NuGet cascades. Every Microsoft.AspNetCore.*, EntityFrameworkCore.*, and Extensions.* package needs version-locked upgrades across the entire dependency graph.
  • Weeks per repository. Manual migration requires reading 50+ pages of breaking change docs per version, then hand-applying each fix.
  • Risk without coverage. Human reviewers miss edge cases. Search recipes flag what automation cannot safely fix.
Compositional Recipes

One recipe. 200+ transformations.

Running UpgradeToDotNet10 on any .NET codebase automatically applies the full chain of migrations — from .NET 3.0 through .NET 10.

Each version-specific composite includes its predecessor. Recipes that have already been applied are no-ops. You don't need to know your starting version.

Auto-fix Search / Flag NuGet
223 individual transformations applied from a single recipe invocation.

Real Results

What this looks like on Billtrust code.

Code Modernization
UseThrowIfNull + ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace

Manual null-check patterns replaced with framework guard methods. The yellow warning flags a TimeProvider modernization opportunity — a search recipe for manual review.

Infrastructure + Modernization
TargetFramework + NuGet + TimeProvider

Target framework net8.0 → net9.0 in csproj, NuGet package upgrades, and DateTime.Now → TimeProvider across test and production code.

These are real diffs from running UpgradeToDotNet10 on Billtrust repositories. Automated refactors in green. Search findings in yellow.
Code Quality

497 recipes across 7 categories.

Every recipe matches an established Roslynator or Meziantou diagnostic. These aren't invented rules — they're the same code quality standards the .NET ecosystem already trusts, implemented as deterministic OpenRewrite transformations.

Style 202
Simplification 88
Performance 73
Redundancy 71
Linq 40
Formatting 13
Naming 10
Tested Against Roslyn

Every recipe is validated against its equivalent Roslyn analyzer on real open source repositories. Formatting differences are treated as bugs.

XUnit → TUnit Migration

9 recipes for complete test framework migration: attribute mapping, lifecycle conversion, data source translation, and dependency management.

Modernization Opt-ins

Optional ModernizeToDotNet9 recipes adopt new APIs: Guid.CreateVersion7, Lock object, RemoveConfigureAwaitFalse.

Engineering Rigor

Every recipe tested against Roslyn.

The Comparison Harness

We built a two-phase harness that compares OpenRewrite recipe output against Roslyn analyzer code fixes on real repositories.

Phase 1 — Baseline
Real repo → Roslyn analyzers → .patch file
Phase 2 — Compare
Real repo → OpenRewrite recipe → Diff vs .patch → .json report
Reports track overreported vs. underreported changes per file. Structured JSON feeds back into automated recipe fixing.
By the Numbers
329
Recipe Commits
202
SDK Commits
10
Days to 500+ Recipes
80+
C# AST Markers
What This Means
  • Formatting-exact output. If a recipe produces different whitespace than Roslyn, we treat it as a bug and fix it.
  • Validated on real code. Not synthetic test cases — real open source repositories with real complexity.
  • Automated feedback loop. Harness JSON reports feed directly into recipe fixes. The harness itself is a recipe development tool.
The SDK

Full C# language support in OpenRewrite.

Foundation Capabilities
  • Roslyn-backed parsing. Full C# syntax support via Roslyn compilation — every language construct from LINQ queries to pattern matching to null-conditional access.
  • Lossless Semantic Tree. Preserves every whitespace character, comment, and formatting choice. Recipes modify the tree; the printer reproduces source-faithful output.
  • CSharpPattern + CSharpTemplate. Semantic matching engine that understands using static, namespace aliases, this. qualification, and commutative operators.
  • AutoFormat pipeline. Roslyn-backed formatting ensures recipe output matches project conventions — not arbitrary defaults.
  • RPC bridge. C# recipes execute within the Java-native Moderne platform via cross-ecosystem RPC. One platform, any language.
  • NuGet workspace. Understands multi-project solutions, shared props, transitive dependencies, and version pinning.
Pattern Matching Example
// Match Console.Write → rewrite to Console.WriteLine var expr = Capture.Expression("expr"); var pat = CSharpPattern.Expression( $"Console.Write({expr})"); var tmpl = CSharpTemplate.Expression( $"Console.WriteLine({expr})"); // In visitor: if (pat.Match(mi, Cursor) is { } match) return (J)tmpl.Apply( Cursor, values: match)!;
Semantic Features
COMMUTATIVE MATCH NULL-CHECK EQUIV USING STATIC TYPED CAPTURES VARIADIC ARGS AUTO-PARENTHESIZATION
Next Steps

Let's prove it on your code.

1. Scope Define target repositories, current .NET version, and target version. Identify pilot candidates.
2. Run Execute UpgradeToDotNet10 + CodeQuality on Billtrust repos. Review generated diffs.
3. Review Diff review, coverage metrics, exception analysis. Measure automated vs. manual effort.
November 2026 is 8 months away. The recipe catalog is ready. The SDK is proven. Let us show you what automated migration looks like on your repositories.
